--- title: "Week 3: Intro to `{purrr}`" subtitle: "& Lab 2" author: "Joe Nese" institute: "University of Oregon
Spring 2026" format: revealjs: theme: default highlight-style: github incremental: false # fig-width: 9 # fig-height: 3.5 scrollable: true slide-number: true execute: echo: true warning: false --- # `{purrr}` & Lab 2 **Week 3** ## Agenda {.smaller} * Review Lab 1 * Thinking more about functional programming + A small example * Introduce you to `{purrr}` and contrast it with the base functions we learned last week **Learning objectives** * Understand how `purrr::map()` relates to `lapply()` and `for` loops * Understand the four basic variants of `purrr::map()`, when they should be used, and when they will fail * Understand what functional programming is, and how `{purrr}` can help facilitate the process # Review Lab 1 # Functional Programming ## What is Functional Programming? > decomposing a big problem into smaller pieces, then solving each piece with a function or combination of functions
\-- [Adv-R](https://adv-r.hadley.nz/fp.html) ## Example Calculate top mpg manufactures (let's just subset the data to 4 cylinders for this example) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(tidyverse) four_cyl <- filter(mpg, cyl == 4) ``` ::: ## Example Now we'll filter for cases where the city miles per gallon is in the top 10% (i.e., greater than or equal to the $90^{th}$ percentile) ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} ninety <- four_cyl |> filter(cty >= quantile(cty, probs = 0.9)) ninety ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 10 × 11 manufacturer model displ year cyl trans drv cty hwy fl class 1 honda civic 1.6 1999 4 manu… f 28 33 r subc… 2 honda civic 1.6 1999 4 manu… f 25 32 r subc… 3 honda civic 1.8 2008 4 manu… f 26 34 r subc… 4 honda civic 1.8 2008 4 auto… f 25 36 r subc… 5 toyota corolla 1.8 1999 4 manu… f 26 35 r comp… 6 toyota corolla 1.8 2008 4 manu… f 28 37 r comp… 7 toyota corolla 1.8 2008 4 auto… f 26 35 r comp… 8 volkswagen jetta 1.9 1999 4 manu… f 33 44 d comp… 9 volkswagen new beetle 1.9 1999 4 manu… f 35 44 d subc… 10 volkswagen new beetle 1.9 1999 4 auto… f 29 41 d subc… ``` ::: ::: ## Example Now let's count the unique occurrences for each manufacturer, manufacturer/model, and class ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} (count_mfr <- count(ninety, manufacturer)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 3 × 2 manufacturer n 1 honda 4 2 toyota 3 3 volkswagen 3 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} (count_model <- count(ninety, manufacturer, model)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 4 × 3 manufacturer model n 1 honda civic 4 2 toyota corolla 3 3 volkswagen jetta 1 4 volkswagen new beetle 2 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} (count_class <- count(ninety, class)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 2 × 2 class n 1 compact 4 2 subcompact 6 ``` ::: ::: ## Example Produce a plot for each ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} plot_mfr <- count_mfr |> ggplot(aes(fct_reorder(manufacturer, -n), n)) + geom_col(aes(fill = manufacturer)) + scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set3") + labs(title = "Manufacturers", x = "", y = "") + guides(fill = "none") plot_mfr ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](w3_files/figure-revealjs/plots1-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} plot_car <- count_model |> unite(car, manufacturer, model, sep = " ") |> ggplot(aes(fct_reorder(car, -n), n)) + geom_col(aes(fill = car)) + scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set3") + labs(title = "Top 10% of city mpg", subtitle = "Car frequency", x = "", y = "") + guides(fill = "none") plot_car ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](w3_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-2-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} plot_class <- count_class |> ggplot(aes(fct_reorder(class, -n), n)) + geom_col(aes(fill = class)) + scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set3") + labs(title = "Car Class", x = "", y = "") + guides(fill = "none") plot_class ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](w3_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Assemble the plots ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(patchwork) plot_car / (plot_mfr + plot_class) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](w3_files/figure-revealjs/patchwork-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: # Functional Programming Version At least in spirit ## Let's Expand from 4 cyl We did a trial run with one piece (4 cyl), now let's do some functional programming and apply it to all `cyl` groups ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} by_cyl <- split(mpg, mpg$cyl) ``` ::: . . . And filter all ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} top_10 <- lapply(by_cyl, function(x) { filter(x, cty >= quantile(cty, probs = 0.9)) }) str(top_10) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` List of 4 $ 4: tibble [10 × 11] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame) ..$ manufacturer: chr [1:10] "honda" "honda" "honda" "honda" ... ..$ model : chr [1:10] "civic" "civic" "civic" chr [1:10] "manual(m5)" "manual(m5)" "manual(m5)" "auto(l5)" ... ..$ drv : chr [1:10] "f" "f" "f" "f" ... ..$ cty : int [1:10] 28 25 26 25 26 28 26 33 35 29 ..$ hwy : int [1:10] 33 32 34 36 35 37 35 chr [1:23] "f" "f" "f" "f" ... ..$ cty : int [1:23] 18 18 18 18 18 18 18 18 19 19 ... ..$ hwy : int [1:23] 26 27 26 29 26 25 26 26 28 27 ... ..$ fl : chr [1:23] "p" "p" "r" "r" ... ..$ class : chr [1:23] "compact" "compact" "midsize" "midsize" ... $ 8: tibble [11 × 11] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame) ..$ manufacturer: chr [1:11] "audi" "chevrolet" "chevrolet" "chevrolet" ... ..$ model : chr [1:11] "a6 quattro" "corvette" "corvette" "corvette" ... ..$ displ : num [1:11] 4.2 5.7 5.7 6.2 6.2 7 4.6 4.6 4.6 4.6 ... ..$ year : int [1:11] 2008 1999 1999 2008 2008 2008 1999 1999 2008 2008 ... ..$ cyl : int [1:11] 8 8 8 8 8 8 8 8 8 8 ... ..$ trans : chr [1:11] "auto(s6)" "manual(m6)" "auto(l4)" "manual(m6)" ... ..$ drv : chr [1:11] "4" "r" "r" "r" ... ..$ cty : int [1:11] 16 16 15 16 15 15 15 15 15 15 ... ..$ hwy : int [1:11] 23 26 23 26 25 24 21 22 23 22 ... ..$ fl : chr [1:11] "p" "p" "p" "p" ... ..$ class : chr [1:11] "midsize" "2seater" "2seater" "2seater" ... ``` ::: ::: ## Counts for all More complex anonymous function here ::: {.cell output-location='slide'} ```{.r .cell-code} counts <- lapply(top_10, function(x) { count_manufacturer <- count(x, manufacturer) count_class <- count(x, class) count_model <- count(x, manufacturer, model) |> unite(car, manufacturer, model, sep = " ") return(list(mfr = count_manufacturer, car = count_model, class = count_class)) }) counts ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` $`4` $`4`$mfr # A tibble: 3 × 2 manufacturer n 1 honda 4 2 toyota 3 3 volkswagen 3 $`4`$car # A tibble: 4 × 2 car n 1 honda civic 4 2 toyota corolla 3 3 volkswagen jetta 1 4 volkswagen new beetle 2 $`4`$class # A tibble: 2 × 2 class n 1 compact 4 2 subcompact 6 $`5` $`5`$mfr # A tibble: 1 × 2 manufacturer n 1 volkswagen 2 $`5`$car # A tibble: 1 × 2 car n 1 volkswagen jetta 2 $`5`$class # A tibble: 1 × 2 class n 1 compact 2 $`6` $`6`$mfr # A tibble: 8 × 2 manufacturer n 1 audi 2 2 chevrolet 2 3 ford 2 4 hyundai 3 5 nissan 5 6 pontiac 2 7 toyota 6 8 volkswagen 1 $`6`$car # A tibble: 10 × 2 car n 1 audi a4 2 2 chevrolet malibu 2 3 ford mustang 2 4 hyundai sonata 3 5 nissan altima 2 6 nissan maxima 3 7 pontiac grand prix 2 8 toyota camry 3 9 toyota camry solara 3 10 volkswagen passat 1 $`6`$class # A tibble: 3 × 2 class n 1 compact 5 2 midsize 16 3 subcompact 2 $`8` $`8`$mfr # A tibble: 4 × 2 manufacturer n 1 audi 1 2 chevrolet 5 3 ford 4 4 pontiac 1 $`8`$car # A tibble: 4 × 2 car n 1 audi a6 quattro 1 2 chevrolet corvette 5 3 ford mustang 4 4 pontiac grand prix 1 $`8`$class # A tibble: 3 × 2 class n 1 2seater 5 2 midsize 2 3 subcompact 4 ``` ::: ::: ## Plots for all {.smaller} Let's write a couple functions (we'll mostly ignore how for now) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} counts_plot <- function(counts_df) { var <- names(counts_df)[1] p <- ggplot(counts_df, aes(fct_reorder(!!sym(var), -n), n)) + geom_col(aes(fill = !!sym(var))) + scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set3") + labs(title = stringr::str_to_title(var), x = "", y = "") + guides(fill = "none") + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)) if(var == "car") { p <- p + labs(title = "Top 10% of city mpg", subtitle = var) } p } ``` ::: ## Test it "4" ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} counts_plot(counts[["4"]]$mfr) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](w3_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-7-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Test it "6" ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} counts_plot(counts[["6"]]$class) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](w3_files/figure-revealjs/indiv_plot3-1.png){width=1248} ::: ::: ## Test it "8" ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} counts_plot(counts[["8"]]$car) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](w3_files/figure-revealjs/indiv_plot2-1.png){width=1248} ::: ::: ## Compile plots function ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} full_plot <- function(l) { counts_plot(l[["car"]]) / ( counts_plot(l[["mfr"]]) + counts_plot(l[["class"]]) ) } ``` ::: ## Test it ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} full_plot(counts[[1]]) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](w3_files/figure-revealjs/full_plot-test-1.png){width=1248} ::: ::: ## Finish up ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} plots <- lapply(counts, full_plot) ``` ::: ## 4 cyl (1) ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} plots[[1]] ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](w3_files/figure-revealjs/p1-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## 5 cyl (2) ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} plots[[2]] ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](w3_files/figure-revealjs/p2-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## 6 cyl (3) ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} plots[[3]] ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](w3_files/figure-revealjs/p3-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## 8 cyl (4) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} plots[[4]] ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](w3_files/figure-revealjs/p4-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: # `{purrr}` ## Functionals **functional** : "a function that takes a function as input and returns a vector as output" . . . You remember from last week and `lapply()` et al. ## `purrr::map()` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(purrr) # loaded automatically with library(tidyverse) map(1:3, rnorm) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [[1]] [1] 1.42172 [[2]] [1] 0.2930833 -1.4425519 [[3]] [1] 1.21714347 1.28169536 -0.04303024 ``` ::: ::: ## Graphically ![](https://d33wubrfki0l68.cloudfront.net/f0494d020aa517ae7b1011cea4c4a9f21702df8b/2577b/diagrams/functionals/map.png) ## Comparison to `base::lapply()` :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} **`lapply()`** ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} lapply(1:3, rnorm) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [[1]] [1] -0.6695783 [[2]] [1] 0.06799364 1.93929808 [[3]] [1] -0.4406266 0.4869425 0.1757094 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} **`map()`** ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} map(1:3, rnorm) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [[1]] [1] 1.417013 [[2]] [1] 0.1988086 2.0793107 [[3]] [1] 1.2627368 -0.2536949 0.5826757 ``` ::: ::: ::: :::: ::: aside side note: What exactly is going on here? ::: ## `map()` & `lapply()` > The base equivalent to `map()` is `lapply()`. The only difference is that `lapply()` does not support the helpers that you’ll learn about (next), so if you’re only using `map()` from `{purrr}`, you can skip the additional dependency and use `lapply()` directly. \- [Adv R](https://adv-r.hadley.nz/functionals.html) ## Equivalents The following are equivalent ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} map(mtcars, function(x) length(unique(x))) lapply(mtcars, function(x) length(unique(x))) ``` ::: ## So why {.smaller} But `{purrr}` also allows you to specify anonymous functions more succinctly using the formula interface . . . From this: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} map(mtcars, function(x) length(unique(x))) ``` ::: To this: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} map(mtcars, ~length(unique(.x))) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` $mpg [1] 25 $cyl [1] 3 $disp [1] 27 $hp [1] 22 $drat [1] 22 $wt [1] 29 $qsec [1] 30 $vs [1] 2 $am [1] 2 $gear [1] 3 $carb [1] 6 ``` ::: ::: . . . If you forget the `~` (I do sometimes!) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} map(mtcars, length(unique(.x))) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error: ! object '.x' not found ``` ::: ::: ## Anonymous function? - An anonymous function is one that is defined without being given a name - Since it has no name assigned with `<-`, it’s anonymous - It’s usually created for short, one-time use—often inside another function like `lapply()`, `sapply()`, `purrr::map()`, etc. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} function(arguments) { # code } ``` ::: # `~` is used in place of `function(x)` ## More examples {.smaller} :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="33%"} Vary the `n` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} map(1:3, ~rnorm(n = .x)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [[1]] [1] 0.08713092 [[2]] [1] -1.010886 1.436855 [[3]] [1] 0.5050764 0.8966680 0.9179680 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="34%"} Vary the `mean` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} map(1:3, ~rnorm(n = 1, mean = .x)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [[1]] [1] 0.3196882 [[2]] [1] 2.361155 [[3]] [1] 2.754484 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="33%"} Vary the `sd` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} map(1:3, ~rnorm(n = 1, sd = .x)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [[1]] [1] 2.191536 [[2]] [1] -0.02042886 [[3]] [1] -1.01078 ``` ::: ::: ::: :::: ## Remember `lapply()` {.smaller} :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="33%"} This doesn't work ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} lapply(1:3, rnorm(x, n = 1)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error: ! object 'x' not found ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="34%"} or this ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} lapply(1:3, ~rnorm(x, n = 1)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in `match.fun()`: ! '~rnorm(x, n = 1)' is not a function, character or symbol ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="33%"} It has to be this ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} lapply(1:3, function(x) rnorm(x, n = 1)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [[1]] [1] 1.10588 [[2]] [1] 3.612761 [[3]] [1] 2.495185 ``` ::: ::: ::: :::: ## Extracting elements Let's make a complicated list ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} (l <- list( list(-1, x = 1, y = 2, z = "a"), list(-2, x = 4, y = c(5, 6), z = "b"), list(-3, x = 8, y = c(9, 10, 11)) )) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [[1]] [[1]][[1]] [1] -1 [[1]]$x [1] 1 [[1]]$y [1] 2 [[1]]$z [1] "a" [[2]] [[2]][[1]] [1] -2 [[2]]$x [1] 4 [[2]]$y [1] 5 6 [[2]]$z [1] "b" [[3]] [[3]][[1]] [1] -3 [[3]]$x [1] 8 [[3]]$y [1] 9 10 11 ``` ::: ::: ## Extract second element from each :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%" .fragment} `map()` 😎 ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} map(l, 2) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [[1]] [1] 1 [[2]] [1] 4 [[3]] [1] 8 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%" .fragment} `lapply` 👎 ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} lapply(l, 2) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in `match.fun()`: ! '2' is not a function, character or symbol ``` ::: ::: ::: :::: ## Doesn't work with `lapply()` Instead, you have to apply an anonymous function ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} lapply(l, function(x) x[[2]]) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [[1]] [1] 1 [[2]] [1] 4 [[3]] [1] 8 ``` ::: ::: . . . Alternatively the following is also the same (and my brain hates it) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} lapply(l, `[[`, 2) ``` ::: ## Extract by name ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} map(l, "y") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [[1]] [1] 2 [[2]] [1] 5 6 [[3]] [1] 9 10 11 ``` ::: ::: ## Multiple arguments Extract first element `1` from `y` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} map(l, list("y", 1)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [[1]] [1] 2 [[2]] [1] 5 [[3]] [1] 9 ``` ::: ::: # `{purrr}` variants ## Return a vector And specify the type * `map_dbl()` * `map_int()` * `map_chr()` * `map_lgl()` ## `l` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} str(l) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` List of 3 $ :List of 4 ..$ : num -1 ..$ x: num 1 ..$ y: num 2 ..$ z: chr "a" $ :List of 4 ..$ : num -2 ..$ x: num 4 ..$ y: num [1:2] 5 6 ..$ z: chr "b" $ :List of 3 ..$ : num -3 ..$ x: num 8 ..$ y: num [1:3] 9 10 11 ``` ::: ::: ## `map_*` :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="33%" .fragment} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} map(l, "x") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [[1]] [1] 1 [[2]] [1] 4 [[3]] [1] 8 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="34%" .fragment} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} map_dbl(l, "x") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 1 4 8 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="33%" .fragment} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} map_dbl(l, 1) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] -1 -2 -3 ``` ::: ::: ::: :::: ## Type Match {.smaller} You'll get an error for coercion ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} map_chr(l, "x") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in `map_chr()`: ℹ In index: 1. Caused by error: ! Can't coerce from a number to a string. ``` ::: ::: . . . You have to explicitly coerce ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} map_chr(l, ~as.character(.x$x)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "1" "4" "8" ``` ::: ::: . . . You'll get an error if element doesn't exist ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} map_chr(l, "z") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in `map_chr()`: ℹ In index: 3. Caused by error: ! Result must be length 1, not 0. ``` ::: ::: . . . Unless you set a default value ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} map_chr(l, "z", .default = NA_character_) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "a" "b" NA ``` ::: ::: ## Quick note: missing values {.smaller} * In the prior case, specifying `NA` would work, instead of `NA_character_` * Generally, I think it's better to specify the type * **General programming rule:** The more strict the better * Because (base) `R` likes to be inconsistent, here are the `NA` types . . . | Type | NA value| |:-----|:-------:| | character | `NA_character_` | | integer | `NA_integer_` | | double | `NA_real_` | | logical | `NA` (see [here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/19564748/why-is-there-no-na-logical)) | . . . ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} typeof(NA) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "logical" ``` ::: ::: ## More quick examples Please copy the code below so you have it locally ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df_list <- list( data.frame(var1 = 1:5), data.frame(var1 = 1:3), data.frame(var1 = 1) ) ``` ::: ## Compute `mean(var1)` For each data frame. You try first! ``{=html} ## Answer ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} map(df_list, ~mean(.x$var1)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [[1]] [1] 3 [[2]] [1] 2 [[3]] [1] 1 ``` ::: ::: . . . Return a vector ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} map_dbl(df_list, ~mean(.x$var1)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 3 2 1 ``` ::: ::: ## Some more examples [**Please follow <script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
